title: Implement Debounce
description: Implement a debounce function that delays execution of a callback until after a specified wait time has elapsed since the last invocation. This is a crucial optimization technique for handling high-frequency events like scroll, resize, and search input.
instructions: Create a function named `debounce` that takes a callback function and wait time in milliseconds
Return a new debounced function that delays execution
Each call should reset the timer if called before wait time expires
Only execute the callback after the wait time has passed without new calls
Preserve the context (this) and arguments from the original call
The debounced function should use clearTimeout to cancel pending executions
learningObjectives: Master closures and their practical applications
Understand asynchronous timing with setTimeout and clearTimeout
Practice preserving function context and arguments
Understand the difference between debounce and throttle

testCases.name: Basic debounce execution
testCases.description: Should execute only once after all calls

testCases.name: Multiple separate debounce windows
testCases.description: Should execute twice for two separate windows

testCases.name: Arguments preservation
testCases.description: Should use arguments from the last call

testCases.name: Rapid successive calls
testCases.description: Should execute only once despite many rapid calls

testCases.name: Long wait time with early calls
testCases.description: Should only execute after final wait period

testCases.name: Debounce with zero wait
testCases.description: Should work with zero wait time

hints.content: Use a closure to store the timeout ID. Each time the debounced function is called, clear any existing timeout and set a new one. This ensures only the last call executes after the wait period.

Store the timeout ID in the outer scope: let timeoutId. In the returned function, use clearTimeout(timeoutId) to cancel pending execution, then use setTimeout to schedule the new execution. Use rest parameters (...args) to capture arguments.

Complete pattern: let timeoutId; return function(...args) { clearTimeout(timeoutId); timeoutId = setTimeout(() => func.apply(this, args), wait); }. The apply method preserves the this context and passes all arguments.
